summary:
Dusk-Eaters are the light-denial forms of Unlife: native negative-vital beings organized around the consumption, inversion, or withholding of twilight, recognition, visibility, warmthless illumination, and the boundary between being seen and being gone.

They are not ordinary shadows, darkness elementals, vampires, night hags, gloom-haunts, or undead that fear sunlight, though they are often mistaken for them. A Dusk-Eater is not defined by living in darkness. It is defined by feeding on the failure of light to complete its work. Dawn that does not arrive, lamps that reveal the wrong thing, twilight that never resolves, watchfires that do not comfort, stars hidden behind omen-cloud, and faces lost at the edge of sight all provide the conditions in which they strengthen.

The defining inversion is that proper illumination diminishes them, while failed, partial, false, or devoured illumination nourishes them. They are strongest at thresholds: sunset, eclipse, storm-darkened noon, fogged lantern-roads, abandoned watchtowers, funeral vigils, caverns with dying phosphorescence, and battlefields where signal fires went unanswered.

Dusk-Eaters are therefore the dusk-facing phylum of Unlife. They do not merely hate light; they corrupt the covenant between light and certainty. They make the visible unreliable, the hidden hungry, and the half-seen more real than the seen.
tax:
Dusk-Eaters belong beneath Unlife because their members are native inverse-vital beings rather than transformed living creatures, undead shadows, or spirits of the dead. They are not cast by bodies, born from corpses, or animated by a former soul. They arise where light, recognition, and passage fail strongly enough to become negative-vital form.

This phylum should be used when the primary mechanism is the inversion of light’s revealing, guiding, warming, or boundary-marking function. A shadow that belongs to a dead creature is not automatically a Dusk-Eater. A vampire hiding from the sun is not a Dusk-Eater. A creature from an underground ecology is not a Dusk-Eater merely because it lives without sunlight. The taxon belongs here when the being feeds on twilight failure, misrecognition, uncompleted dawn, devoured signals, false lamps, or the fear that comes from light revealing too little.

Dusk-Eaters are especially associated with liminal light: dusk, dawn delayed, dying embers, moonlit uncertainty, lantern haze, candle gutters, auroras, witchlight, corpse-glow, and the last visible outline before darkness. Their outward form is secondary. A specimen may appear as a moth-like giant, a long-limbed watcher, a lamp-headed beggar, a black bird at sunset, a many-eyed fog, a faceless guard, a cloak of dimness, or a child-shaped silhouette at the road’s edge. It belongs here only if its persistence is rooted in light being consumed or made untrustworthy.
ap:
Dusk-Eaters usually appear as figures made from the last usable light in a place. Their bodies may be smoky at the edges, blue-black, ember-veined, moth-winged, star-specked, or outlined in a dim halo that makes them harder rather than easier to see. Many have eyes like covered lanterns, mouths filled with fireflies, fingers ending in wick-black claws, or cloaks that flutter like extinguished flame.

Some are almost invisible in full darkness and full daylight, becoming most coherent in uncertain light. At dusk, a Dusk-Eater may stand where a tree’s shadow lengthens. At dawn, it may appear as the one dark shape not shortened by the sun. In torchlight, it may bend toward the flame without casting a normal shadow. In moonlight, it may show the wrong face.

Field signs include lamps that dim without smoke, torches burning cold, shadows leaning toward the light source, signal fires seen by the wrong watchers, stars disappearing one by one above a single road, reflective eyes in places too high for animals, and silhouettes remaining after the body has moved. A candle near a Dusk-Eater may illuminate dust, walls, and weapons clearly, but fail to show the face of the person holding it.

Witnesses report uncertainty more often than blindness. They can see, but cannot trust what sight gives them. Companions appear unfamiliar at the edge of the lantern. Doors look open until touched. Roads fork under moonlight and become one path at noon. Campfires warm hands but not courage. Watchmen see approaching shapes but cannot count them. A victim may look directly at a Dusk-Eater and remember only that the light was bad.
eco:
Dusk-Eaters persist where light repeatedly fails to guide, reveal, warn, warm, or conclude a passage. Their habitats include twilight roads, abandoned watchposts, ruined lighthouses or signal towers, fog-bound coasts, deep forests, cavern mouths, eclipse sites, battlefield ridges, old borders, haunted crossroads, siege camps, dying hearths, moonlit grave roads, and settlements where night watches failed.

They do not reproduce by mating or ordinary shadow-casting. A Dusk-Eater forms when twilight failure becomes patterned: a beacon never lit, a watchfire ignored, a dawn delayed by curse or fear, travelers lost because a lamp lied, a city gate opened to the wrong silhouettes, a vigil abandoned before morning, or a community that learns to distrust every light after repeated betrayals.

Lesser Dusk-Eaters often begin as environmental anomalies: a patch of dimness that eats torchlight, a sunset that lingers too long in one alley, a lantern reflection with teeth, a road-marker visible only when walked away from, or a swarm of black moths around a cold flame. Greater specimens develop bodies, territories, hunting habits, and recognizable hungers.

Their ecology is fed by failed illumination. False beacons, cold lamps, obscured stars, neglected hearths, watchfires that no one answers, holy lights used to deceive, and torches carried by lost or faithless guides all strengthen them. Conversely, true dawn, named constellations, honest signals, shared campfires, consecrated lamps, hearths relit by rightful hands, and light used to reveal rather than deceive can weaken them.

Dusk-Eaters often cluster around thresholds. They favor gates at sunset, roads between settlements, forest edges, cave mouths, passes, bridges, shorelines, city walls, and the last lit window before wilderness. They are less interested in endless night than in the moment when light should have carried someone safely across a boundary and failed.
beh:
Dusk-Eaters behave like predators of uncertainty. They do not always extinguish lights outright. More often they bend, thin, redirect, chill, or falsify them. A torch still burns, but shows the wrong road. A lantern reveals a friend as a stranger. A signal fire appears on the wrong hill. A candle allows a reader to see every word except the warning.

Many hunt by delay. They lengthen dusk, make dawn feel distant, cause watches to miss the decisive moment, or keep travelers moving until every landmark has become doubtful. Others hunt by substitution, placing a false light where a true one was expected. The victim follows, not because they are blind, but because they trust the wrong illumination.

Dusk-Eaters are often fascinated by sentries, guides, priests of light, astronomers, scouts, mourners keeping vigil, lighthouse keepers, torchbearers, miners, caravan leaders, and children afraid of the dark. They may stalk anyone responsible for helping others see or pass safely through danger.

Intelligent specimens cultivate rituals of mistrust. They teach settlements to shutter windows before sunset, punish those who carry lamps, spread stories that dawn itself is unsafe, or replace signal codes with omens only they can interpret. Some present themselves as guides through darkness, offering safe passage in exchange for a lantern, a memory of sunrise, a name spoken at dusk, or the victim’s trust in their own sight.

Dusk-Eaters rarely value total darkness as an end state. Total darkness starves many of their preferred games. Their richest feeding comes from imperfect seeing: the half-lit corridor, the dim watchfire, the false star, the friend glimpsed as monster, the road visible only until it matters.
traitsText:
**Type Guidance**. Use Unlife as the preferred type. If a ruleset lacks Unlife, use Undead with a native-unlife, dusk, shadow, negative-vital, or light-eating subtype. Dusk-Eaters are mechanically adjacent to shadows, darkness creatures, light-sensitive undead, and gloom haunts, but they are not automatically any of those.

**Light-Inversion Core**. A Dusk-Eater should be defined by a failed function of light: guidance, revelation, warning, warmth, witness, signal, boundary, dawn, dusk, or recognition. Do not define it only as “a monster that likes darkness.”

**Threshold Ecology**. Dusk-Eaters are strongest at boundaries of illumination and passage. Lower entries should specify whether they prefer dusk, false dawn, eclipse, moonlight, dying fire, cold lanterns, cave mouths, gates, roads, or watchfires.

**Senses**. Darkvision is common, but dusk-sight, low-light vision, blindsense in dim light, lifesense through silhouettes, the ability to detect active light sources, or the ability to sense creatures relying on vision may be more distinctive.

**Defenses**. Concealment in dim light, resistance to cold or negative energy, channel resistance, immunity to blindness, fatigue, sleep, disease, poison, or nonlethal damage may fit many descendants. These defenses should weaken under true daylight, consecrated lamps, honest signal fires, named stars, or light produced by fulfilled vows.

**Weakness Pattern**. Proper illumination harms or exposes Dusk-Eaters. A lower entry should define what counts: sunrise seen directly, a lamp lit by a rightful keeper, a signal answered correctly, a hearth relit for guests, a holy light that reveals truth, a named star used for navigation, or a torch shared rather than hoarded. The effect should be bounded: forced visibility, loss of concealment, suppressed aura, reduced mobility, damage, staggered condition, or temporary banishment.

**Skills**. Stealth, Perception, Survival, Bluff, Disguise, Intimidate, Sense Motive, Knowledge geography, Knowledge nature, Knowledge religion, Knowledge planes, and Perform may be appropriate. Bonuses should follow the light-failure: road-lurers use Survival and Bluff, lantern-forms use Stealth and Disguise, signal-eaters use Perception and Knowledge geography.

**Environment**. Twilight roads, borders, forests, cave mouths, ruins, watchtowers, signal posts, coastlines, mountain passes, moonlit grave roads, fog-bound settlements, siege camps, and any place where a light once failed its duty.

**Organization**. Solitary, pair, watch, swarm, lantern-line, false dawn, moth-clutch, signal knot, dusk court, cold vigil, or procession.
abilities:
**Eat Light** (Su). A Dusk-Eater can reduce, chill, thin, or consume illumination within a defined area. This may lower light levels, suppress mundane flames, weaken magical light after a caster-level check, or convert bright light into dim light. The ability should not create unlimited darkness without cost or limit.

**False Lantern** (Sp or Su). The Dusk-Eater creates a misleading light source: a campfire where no camp exists, a lantern on the wrong road, a signal from the wrong tower, or a familiar window shining from an impossible direction. Creatures who follow the light may become lost, separated, fascinated, or drawn into an ambush. Clear maps, true stars, or trusted companions may grant bonuses to resist.

**Dusk Aura** (Su). Living creatures near a strong Dusk-Eater may suffer uncertainty in dim light. Penalties may apply to Perception, Survival, Sense Motive, ranged attacks, identification checks, or saving throws against fear and illusion. Severe versions may cause shaken, dazzled, fascinated, or confused conditions, but the effect should remain tied to unreliable sight.

**Unfinished Sunset** (Su). The Dusk-Eater can prolong a local dusk-state, preventing an area from becoming fully day or fully night for a short period. This empowers its own dim-light traits and may interfere with effects keyed to sunrise, sunset, or daily renewal. The ability should be localized and bounded.

**Silhouette Step** (Su). In dim light, the Dusk-Eater may move between shadows, silhouettes, lantern edges, or elongated dusk-shapes. This can function like short-range teleportation, concealment-assisted movement, or a shadow step. Bright truthful light or total darkness may interrupt the path, depending on the descendant.

**Cold Watchfire** (Su). A Dusk-Eater can cause a fire to shed light without warmth, or warmth without reassurance. Creatures resting near such a fire may fail to recover fully, suffer fear penalties, or become easier to lure. A properly tended communal hearth should suppress this effect.

**Misrecognition Glimmer** (Su). The Dusk-Eater alters what light reveals. Allies may briefly appear as enemies, exits as walls, wounds as harmless stains, or monsters as familiar companions. This should allow Will saves and should be weaker against creatures using senses other than sight.

**True Dawn Diminishment** (Su). When exposed to proper revelatory light, the Dusk-Eater loses coherence. Effects may include damage, loss of concealment, forced materialization, suppressed fast healing, inability to use shadow movement, reduced aura strength, or temporary banishment. The required light should be defined by lower taxon: sunrise, consecrated lantern, answered signal fire, named star, or restored hearth.

**Devour Signal** (Su). A Dusk-Eater may consume or redirect a warning light, beacon, flare, spell-signal, or watchfire message. The signal may vanish, appear elsewhere, arrive late, or be understood incorrectly. This ability is strongest in places where watchers already fear betrayal or abandonment.
bg:
Dusk-Eaters give Unlife a phylum for monsters of failed light rather than simple darkness. Their horror lies in the moment sight should save someone and instead betrays them.

They fit stories about lost caravans following false lamps, watchtowers whose signals go unanswered, towns where sunset lasts too long, miners guided by cold witchlights, sentries who cannot count the shapes outside the wall, or heroes who realize the campfire has been showing them the wrong faces all night.

They are useful in 3.5e and Pathfinder-style play because they can support both tactical and investigative encounters. In combat, they manipulate light levels, concealment, fear, and positioning. In exploration, they threaten navigation, watches, signal systems, rest, and trust in visual evidence. In mystery play, the question is not merely “where is the monster hiding?” but “which light has been lying?”

The practical field lesson is that more light is not always enough. A bigger torch may feed the wrong effect if the flame is false, stolen, or mistrusted. The correct answer is proper light: the right beacon relit, the true path marked, the watch resumed, the hearth shared, the dawn witnessed, the star named, or the signal answered.

Misclassification is dangerous. Treating a Dusk-Eater as a generic shadow may lead hunters to flood an area with careless light, creating more false reflections and longer silhouettes. Treating it as an undead sun-fearer may miss the fact that it does not merely flee day; it feeds on the failure of day to arrive, reveal, or reassure.
note:
Theme: darkness as inverted sunlight.
Name root: plain English/fantasy, suitable for field taxonomy.

Description: Dusk-Eaters are Unlife organized around light-inversion. They may crave light, color, dawn, recognition, or visibility, but light diminishes them. They grow in darkness, shadow, obscurity, forgetting, and unlit thresholds.

Supportive argument: This preserves the repo’s Shadow Creatures branch, but reframes it through inverse appetite rather than “shadow animals.” They are not simply creatures that like darkness; they are beings whose desire for light produces deeper dark.
